findings.summary: Single Settings class in common/config.py mixes OCR-specific and classifier-specific configuration into one 30+ field hub, coupling both daemons to all settings
evidence: Settings loads OCR fields (OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS, OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS, REFUSAL_MARK) and classifier fields (CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID, CLASSIFY_MAX_CHARS, CLASSIFY_TAG_LIMIT, CLASSIFY_MAX_PAGES, etc.) in the same __init__
The classifier daemon instantiates Settings and validates OCR_DPI/PAGE_WORKERS it never uses; the OCR daemon validates CLASSIFY_MAX_CHARS/CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID it never uses
Any change to a classifier-only env var could break the OCR daemon's startup if validation logic changes
findings.suggestion: Split into a base CommonSettings plus OcrSettings and ClassifierSettings subclasses or composition objects, so each daemon only loads and validates the fields it needs

findings.summary: common/tags.py pipeline_tag_ids() and clean_pipeline_tags() hardcode knowledge of every pipeline's tag IDs, coupling the shared module to both domain-specific tag schemes
evidence: pipeline_tag_ids() at tags.py:151-162 explicitly references settings.PRE_TAG_ID, settings.POST_TAG_ID, settings.CLASSIFY_PRE_TAG_ID, settings.OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID, settings.CLASSIFY_PROCESSING_TAG_ID, settings.CLASSIFY_POST_TAG_ID, and settings.ERROR_TAG_ID
Adding a new pipeline stage would require modifying this shared function rather than just the new pipeline's own code
findings.suggestion: Accept the set of pipeline tag IDs as a parameter rather than reaching into Settings for every pipeline's IDs, letting each daemon specify which tags to clean

findings.summary: Module-level mutable singleton in common/llm.py for the OpenAI client couples initialization order to runtime correctness with no compile-time safety
evidence: _openai_holder is a module-level _OpenAIClientHolder instance that must be initialized via init_openai_client() before any provider calls get_openai_client()
Correct ordering depends entirely on bootstrap_daemon() calling setup_libraries() before run_preflight_checks()
preflight.py calls get_openai_client() which raises RuntimeError if the initialization order is wrong
findings.suggestion: Pass the OpenAI client explicitly through bootstrap_daemon's return value and into providers, removing the global singleton pattern

findings.summary: common/library_setup.py unconditionally imports and configures Pillow (Image.MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS = None) for both daemons, even though only the OCR daemon uses images
evidence: library_setup.py line 20: Image.MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS = None runs during classifier bootstrap too
from PIL import Image is a top-level import in the shared module, pulling Pillow into the classifier's import graph
findings.suggestion: Move the Pillow configuration into ocr-specific bootstrap or guard behind a conditional check on which daemon is running

findings.summary: The common/ package has 15 modules in a flat directory mixing infrastructure (logging, shutdown, retry, concurrency) with domain coordination (tags, claims, document_iter, stale_lock)
evidence: 15 Python modules at the same directory level with no sub-grouping
Infrastructure concerns (retry.py, shutdown.py, logging_config.py, concurrency.py) sit alongside domain-specific coordination modules (tags.py, claims.py, document_iter.py, stale_lock.py)
For a project of ~40 total files this is borderline acceptable but reduces navigability
findings.suggestion: Consider grouping into sub-packages like common/infra/ (retry, shutdown, logging_config, concurrency, library_setup) if the project grows further

findings.summary: Pillow and pdf2image are top-level project dependencies but are only used by the OCR daemon, adding unnecessary weight for classifier-only installations
evidence: Pillow is imported in ocr/image_converter.py, ocr/provider.py, ocr/worker.py, and common/library_setup.py
pdf2image is only imported in ocr/image_converter.py: 'from pdf2image import convert_from_bytes'
pdf2image requires poppler-utils as a system dependency
The classifier daemon never processes images but pays the installation cost for both libraries
findings.suggestion: Move Pillow and pdf2image to an optional dependency group (e.g., [project.optional-dependencies] ocr = ['Pillow~=10.4', 'pdf2image~=1.17']) so the classifier can be installed without them

findings.summary: Both httpx and openai are pinned as direct dependencies; openai internally depends on httpx, creating a potential version conflict surface
evidence: pyproject.toml declares httpx~=0.28 and openai~=1.35
httpx is used directly in PaperlessClient and also passed to the OpenAI SDK via httpx.Client(trust_env=False) in library_setup.py
Both httpx usages are justified -- this is not duplication -- but the ~=0.28 pin could conflict with openai's internal httpx dependency in future openai releases
findings.suggestion: Monitor openai release notes for httpx version bumps; consider loosening httpx constraint to >=0.28,<1.0 or letting openai drive the httpx version

findings.summary: Single Settings class owns configuration for both OCR and classifier daemons, coupling unrelated subsystems at the config layer
evidence: Settings.__init__ unconditionally calls _load_classification_settings and _load_image_settings - the OCR daemon loads CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID, CLASSIFY_MAX_CHARS, CLASSIFY_TAG_LIMIT etc. that it never uses, and vice versa for the classifier loading OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS
The class has 35+ attributes spanning both domains with no separation
findings.suggestion: Split into a base SharedSettings plus OcrSettings and ClassifierSettings subclasses so each daemon only loads and validates its own configuration

findings.summary: The common/ package contains 15 modules spanning HTTP client, retry, concurrency, tag lifecycle, claim protocol, shutdown, bootstrap, preflight, logging, and document iteration with blurred ownership boundaries
evidence: tags.py, claims.py, stale_lock.py, and document_iter.py form a cohesive tag-lifecycle cluster but live alongside unrelated infrastructure modules (retry.py, concurrency.py, shutdown.py, logging_config.py)
A new engineer cannot predict what lives in common/ without reading the directory listing
findings.suggestion: Consider sub-packaging: common/pipeline/ for tag lifecycle (tags, claims, stale_lock, document_iter) and keeping infra modules (retry, concurrency, shutdown) at the top level

findings.summary: Both daemons follow an identical worker/daemon/provider decomposition with predictable ownership - a strong structural positive
evidence: Both packages have daemon.py (entry point + polling setup), worker.py (per-document orchestrator class), and provider.py (LLM interaction with model fallback)
Both use bootstrap_daemon -> run_polling_threadpool -> process_document with identical lifecycle patterns
findings.suggestion: No action needed - this is a strong positive that makes the codebase predictable

findings.summary: Tags are repeatedly converted between set[int] and list[int] at every boundary crossing, creating noisy translation code throughout the pipeline
evidence: extract_tags returns set[int] but update_document_metadata and update_document accept list[int]
list(tags) or list(updated) appears at least 10 times: tags.py:67, tags.py:107, claims.py:53, worker.py:200, classifier/worker.py:196, classifier/worker.py:299, stale_lock.py:46
findings.suggestion: Have PaperlessClient accept Iterable[int] for tags and do the list conversion internally, eliminating redundant list() calls at every call site

findings.summary: TaxonomyCache holds its RLock during Paperless HTTP API calls, blocking all concurrent classification workers during potentially slow network operations
evidence: refresh() at line 136-151 holds self._lock while making three sequential paginated API calls (list_correspondents, list_document_types, list_tags)
_get_or_create_item_id at lines 186-209 holds self._lock during kind.creator() HTTP POST calls, and also calls self.refresh() on failure while already holding the lock
findings.suggestion: Use a read-copy-update pattern: fetch data outside the lock, then swap the indices atomically inside the lock. For creation, release the lock during the HTTP call and re-check after

findings.summary: The retry decorator implicitly requires the decorated method's class to have a settings attribute conforming to RetrySettings protocol, creating a hidden coupling contract
evidence: retry.py line 32-33: wrapper(self, *args, **kwargs) silently assumes self.settings exists and satisfies RetrySettings
RetrySettings protocol exists but is never checked at decoration time; an AttributeError at runtime is the only enforcement
findings.suggestion: Accept retry parameters explicitly as decorator arguments or add a clear error message when the protocol is not satisfied

findings.summary: The OpenAI client singleton requires init() before get(), creating temporal coupling enforced only at runtime with a cryptic RuntimeError
evidence: _OpenAIClientHolder.get() raises RuntimeError if _client is None
init is done in library_setup.setup_libraries() called by bootstrap_daemon(); any test or alternative entry point that forgets this ordering gets a cryptic error
findings.suggestion: Pass the OpenAI client as a constructor dependency to providers rather than relying on module-level singleton state with ordering requirements

findings.summary: Both daemons create and destroy a PaperlessClient (HTTP session) per document, losing connection pooling benefits
evidence: ocr/daemon.py _process_document creates PaperlessClient(settings) per document and closes in finally
classifier/daemon.py process_document does the same - TCP connection setup/teardown per document
findings.suggestion: Use a thread-local persistent client or share a single httpx.Client (which is thread-safe) across workers

findings.summary: Error handling at module boundaries mixes swallowed exceptions, return-False, return-None, and sentinel-value patterns without a consistent strategy
evidence: release_processing_tag swallows all errors (best-effort)
claim_processing_tag returns False on any error
ClassificationProvider._create_with_compat returns None on API errors
OcrProvider.transcribe_image returns REFUSAL_MARK sentinel on total failure
finalize_document_with_error lets exceptions propagate
findings.suggestion: Document and formalize the error strategy per boundary; consider a Result type or explicit classification of hard vs soft failures

findings.summary: DocumentProcessor.process() in both workers has high cyclomatic complexity with 60+ line try/finally blocks tracking claimed/success state
evidence: OCR worker.process() has a try block spanning lines 63-119 with nested try/except for image conversion, conditional error-tag checks, claimed/success boolean state tracking, and a finally block at lines 120-135
Classifier worker.process() similarly spans lines 84-144 with multiple early returns for empty content, generic types, error tags, plus claimed state management
findings.suggestion: Extract the claim-process-release lifecycle into a context manager that handles the claimed/finally pattern, reducing process() to a linear pipeline of steps

findings.summary: OCR DocumentProcessor.process() computes elapsed time after the finally block, so it is never logged on error paths
evidence: start_time set at line 60, try block at line 63, finally at lines 120-128, elapsed computation at lines 129-135

If an exception propagates, lines 129-135 are skipped since they are inside the finally block but after the release_processing_tag and _log_ocr_stats calls - actually they ARE inside the finally block, so this is fine
findings.suggestion: On re-examination the elapsed logging IS inside the finally block (lines 128-135). No action needed - this finding was initially incorrect and is retracted.

findings.summary: OCR error/refusal detection uses overlapping but inconsistent marker sets across different detection sites
evidence: provider.py uses is_error_content(text, self.settings.OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS) with the full configurable marker list
worker.py _has_ocr_errors checks OCR_ERROR_MARKER OR is_error_content(text, self.settings.OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS)
classifier/quality_gates.py needs_error_tag uses is_error_content(text, REFUSAL_PHRASES) from constants, a different marker set
findings.suggestion: Unify error content detection with a single canonical marker set, or document why different stages intentionally check different subsets

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider._create_with_compat uses a while-True loop with counter-based termination instead of bounded iteration
evidence: Lines 79-97: while True with compat_retries counter compared against len(_COMPAT_PARAMS), three exit paths (return response, continue after strip, return None)
findings.suggestion: Refactor to a bounded for loop (for _ in range(len(self._COMPAT_PARAMS) + 1)) making the maximum iteration count explicit in the loop structure

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider._supports_temperature uses hardcoded model name prefix that will silently break as model naming evolves
evidence: Line 45: 'return not model.startswith("gpt-5")' - future gpt-5.x variants might support temperature, and models like o1/o3 that also lack temperature support would incorrectly get temperature set
The compat retry mechanism exists as a safety net, making this check redundant
findings.suggestion: Remove the prefix check and rely entirely on the compat retry mechanism, or make unsupported-temperature model patterns configurable

findings.summary: Processing tag ID checks use falsy tests ('if not tag_id') instead of explicit None checks, conflating 0 with absent
evidence: claims.py line 26: 'if not tag_id: return True'
tags.py line 90: 'if not tag_id: return'
document_iter.py line 63: 'if processing_tag_id and processing_tag_id in tags'
Type annotations say 'int | None' but checks treat 0 and None identically
findings.suggestion: Use 'is None' checks to match the type annotation; config already converts <=0 to None but the runtime checks should not depend on that

findings.summary: Settings class bundles 30+ fields spanning OCR, classification, daemon, image, and logging config into one object passed everywhere
evidence: Settings has fields for OCR (OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS), classification (CLASSIFY_MAX_CHARS, CLASSIFY_TAG_LIMIT, CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID), daemon (POLL_INTERVAL, MAX_RETRIES), and logging (LOG_LEVEL, LOG_FORMAT) -- all in one class
OcrProvider only needs AI_MODELS, OCR_MAX_SIDE, REQUEST_TIMEOUT, OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS, REFUSAL_MARK but receives the full Settings object
ClassificationProvider similarly only uses AI_MODELS, REQUEST_TIMEOUT, LLM_PROVIDER, CLASSIFY_MAX_TOKENS, CLASSIFY_TAG_LIMIT from Settings
findings.suggestion: Split Settings into focused config groups (LLMConfig, OCRConfig, ClassifyConfig, DaemonConfig) or use typed subsets so consumers declare their actual dependencies

findings.summary: OCR worker class is DocumentProcessor while classifier worker is ClassificationProcessor, obscuring their parallel roles
evidence: src/ocr/worker.py defines class DocumentProcessor
src/classifier/worker.py defines class ClassificationProcessor
Both serve identical structural roles: per-document processing orchestrator instantiated per-document by the daemon thread pool
findings.suggestion: Rename OCR's DocumentProcessor to OcrProcessor for symmetry, or adopt a shared convention like {Domain}Processor

findings.summary: OCR daemon uses a module-level function for per-document processing while classifier daemon uses an inline closure, despite identical logic
evidence: src/ocr/daemon.py lines 18-26: _process_document is a module-level function taking (doc, settings)
src/classifier/daemon.py lines 66-75: process_document is a local closure inside main() capturing settings, taxonomy_cache from enclosing scope
Both follow the same pattern: create PaperlessClient, create provider, instantiate processor, call process(), close client in finally
findings.suggestion: Use the same pattern in both daemons -- either both module-level or both closures. The classifier closure is justified by the extra taxonomy_cache dependency, but the structural divergence is worth noting

findings.summary: Both workers duplicate the error-tag early-exit check, claim/release lifecycle, and stats logging pattern
evidence: src/ocr/worker.py lines 68-71: checks ERROR_TAG_ID in current_tags, calls _finalize_with_error, returns
src/classifier/worker.py lines 89-95: identical check and early return
Both workers also have identical patterns for: claimed = claim_processing_tag(...), if not claimed: return, finally: if claimed: release_processing_tag(...)
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared processing lifecycle (error check, claim, release, stats) into a base class or a context-manager/callback protocol, leaving domain logic to subclasses

findings.summary: OpenAIChatMixin provides only _create_completion, acting as inheritance-based dependency injection for a single function
evidence: OpenAIChatMixin has exactly one method: _create_completion that reads module-global _openai_holder and wraps with llm_limiter
The @retry decorator on it implicitly requires self.settings to satisfy RetrySettings protocol -- a hidden contract
Both OcrProvider and ClassificationProvider inherit from it solely to get this one method
findings.suggestion: Replace the mixin with a composed LLMClient class injected via constructor, or a standalone function taking explicit retry config

findings.summary: is_openai_client_ready accesses _openai_holder._client directly, bypassing the holder's encapsulation
evidence: Line 55: 'return _openai_holder._client is not None' reaches into the private _client attribute instead of using a method
findings.suggestion: Add an is_ready() method to _OpenAIClientHolder and call that from is_openai_client_ready

findings.summary: The retry decorator implicitly requires self.settings on decorated methods via runtime attribute access
evidence: retry.py line 33: 'settings: RetrySettings = self.settings' -- the decorator reaches into self to find settings
RetrySettings is a Protocol but the coupling is invisible at the decoration site; any class using @retry must have a conforming self.settings
findings.suggestion: Accept a settings accessor or pass max_retries/max_backoff directly to the decorator, making the contract visible at decoration time

findings.summary: classifier/ and ocr/ __init__.py re-export key classes with __all__, but common/ __init__.py exports nothing
evidence: src/classifier/__init__.py imports and exports 5 symbols via __all__
src/ocr/__init__.py imports and exports 5 symbols via __all__
src/common/__init__.py contains only a docstring with no re-exports
All actual callers use direct imports from submodules (e.g. from common.config import Settings) -- the __init__.py facades appear unused internally
findings.suggestion: Either add consistent __all__ exports to common/__init__.py or remove them from domain packages since callers use direct submodule imports. Unused facades can become stale

findings.summary: Exception types caught at I/O boundaries vary across modules without a shared constant or taxonomy
evidence: tags.py, claims.py, stale_lock.py all catch (OSError, httpx.HTTPError) at their call boundaries
paperless.py defines RETRYABLE_HTTP_EXCEPTIONS = (httpx.RequestError, httpx.HTTPStatusError) which is a different tuple used for the retry decorator
daemon_loop.py line 106 catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError) -- a broader superset
No shared constant for the 'catch at call boundary' exception tuple, so each new call site must independently decide which exceptions to catch
findings.suggestion: Define a PAPERLESS_CATCHABLE_EXCEPTIONS constant in common/ and use it consistently across modules that call PaperlessClient methods, reducing the chance of drift

findings.summary: Preflight LLM check calls get_openai_client().models.list() which uses the properly configured client, but the import and guard pattern differs from other client access points
evidence: preflight.py imports both get_openai_client and is_openai_client_ready from llm.py
is_openai_client_ready uses private attribute access (_openai_holder._client is not None) while get_openai_client uses the public .get() method
This creates an asymmetric guard: the readiness check bypasses encapsulation but the actual usage respects it
findings.suggestion: Add a proper is_ready() method to _OpenAIClientHolder to make both paths consistent

findings.summary: At least 8 test files define their own local _make_settings() wrapper that simply delegates to the centralized make_settings_obj() factory. This is boilerplate duplication that obscures which test files use shared infrastructure and which have genuinely custom setup.
evidence: test_worker.py line 37: 'def _make_settings(**overrides): from tests.helpers.factories import make_settings_obj; return make_settings_obj(**overrides)'. Identical pattern in test_provider.py:34, test_daemon.py:29, test_paperless.py:26, test_ocr_workflow.py:32, test_classifier_workflow.py:25. Similarly, _make_mock_paperless wrappers in test_worker.py:42 and test_daemon.py:34 just forward to make_mock_paperless. These wrappers add zero logic.
findings.suggestion: Import make_settings_obj and make_mock_paperless directly where needed, or define them as shared pytest fixtures in conftest.py. The pass-through wrappers add indirection without value.

findings.summary: Tests for worker classes extensively patch internal private methods by name (e.g., _ocr_pages_in_parallel, _update_paperless_document, _finalize_with_error), making them fragile to any refactoring of the worker's internal decomposition.
evidence: test_worker.py patches private methods like '_ocr_pages_in_parallel', '_update_paperless_document', and '_finalize_with_error' as test boundaries. These are implementation details of DocumentProcessor. Renaming or inlining any of these private methods breaks multiple tests even if external behavior is unchanged.
findings.suggestion: Where possible, test through the public process() method with controlled mocks at the real external boundaries (PaperlessClient, OcrProvider) rather than patching internal private methods. Reserve private method testing for complex internal logic that warrants isolation.

findings.summary: The thread-pool concurrency code in daemon_loop.py has no tests for race conditions, shutdown during active processing, or thread pool exhaustion. Tests mock the executor or test single-threaded behavior only.
evidence: daemon_loop.py uses ThreadPoolExecutor with as_completed for concurrent document processing. test_daemon_loop.py tests _process_batch, _poll_once, and run_polling_threadpool but uses synchronous process_item callables that complete immediately. No test verifies behavior when items are processed concurrently with competing threads, or when shutdown is requested while items are in-flight in the pool.
findings.suggestion: Add integration-level tests that use real ThreadPoolExecutor with concurrent work items and verify correct behavior under shutdown signals and item failures happening simultaneously.

findings.summary: test_config.py hardcodes expected default values (tag IDs, timeouts, model lists) that mirror the source. When a default changes in config.py, the test must be updated in lockstep, but the test does not verify any behavior -- just that a constant equals another constant.
evidence: test_config.py line 47: 'assert s.PAPERLESS_URL == "http://paperless:8000"'. Line 55: 'assert s.AI_MODELS == ["gpt-5-mini", "gpt-5.2", "o4-mini"]'. These directly restate the default values from config.py lines 10-11 and 100. Changes to defaults require updating both files, and the tests do not verify any interesting behavior (validation, parsing, override logic).
findings.suggestion: Focus Settings default tests on behavioral properties (e.g., 'default AI_MODELS has at least one entry', 'default POLL_INTERVAL is positive') rather than pinning exact default values. Test override and validation logic instead.

findings.summary: Test files begin with large docstrings that enumerate every test case in the file. These docstrings restate the test class and method names, adding maintenance burden without providing information that the test names don't already convey.
evidence: test_worker.py lines 1-20: A 20-line docstring listing every test scenario ('process() happy path: full pipeline success', 'process() claim fails -> returns early', etc.). test_provider.py lines 1-16: Similar enumeration. These are maintenance liabilities -- adding or removing a test requires updating the docstring, and they are never rendered in test output. The test method names already describe each scenario.
findings.suggestion: Replace exhaustive test-listing docstrings with a brief one-liner stating what module is under test. Let the test names speak for themselves.

findings.summary: Tests are organized into single-method classes (one class per scenario) throughout the test suite. This pattern inflates file sizes, adds indentation overhead, and produces no benefit over flat functions when the classes share no state or fixtures.
evidence: test_config.py uses classes like TestDefaults, TestPaperlessUrl, TestLlmProvider, etc., each containing a handful of flat test methods that share no setup, fixtures, or state. Most classes have no __init__, no class fixtures, no setUp -- they just namespace tests that could be top-level functions. This is consistent across nearly every test file in the project.
findings.suggestion: Use classes only when tests share fixtures or setup (via class-scoped fixtures or setUp). For isolated test functions, flat module-level test functions are simpler and more Pythonic.

findings.summary: Source and test files use heavy ASCII banner comments (lines of dashes, equals signs) to separate logical sections. These visual separators add noise and are hallmarks of AI-generated code structure.
findings.suggestion: Remove banner comments. Python code should be organized by blank lines and clear naming. If a file needs heavy visual separation, it may need to be split into smaller modules instead.

findings.summary: Several trivial functions have multi-line docstrings that restate what the code does without adding any insight about why or what constraints apply.
evidence: llm.py line 46: 'def init_openai_client(client: openai.OpenAI) -> None:' with docstring 'Store the configured OpenAI client for use by the mixin.' -- the function name already says this. llm.py line 67: 'def unique_models(models: list[str]) -> list[str]:' with docstring 'Deduplicate a model list while preserving insertion order.' -- also self-evident. test_provider.py line 49: '_make_response' with docstring 'Create a mock OpenAI chat completion response.' -- restates the function name. test_provider.py line 57: '_make_test_image' with docstring 'Create a non-blank test image.' -- obvious from the function name and body.
findings.suggestion: Remove docstrings from functions whose names and signatures already communicate their purpose. Reserve docstrings for non-obvious behavior, constraints, or side effects.

findings.summary: PaperlessClient has debug-level log lines on nearly every method entry point, logging the URL for routine operations. This entry/exit logging pattern on straightforward HTTP wrapper methods is characteristic of AI-generated code.
evidence: paperless.py line 124: log.debug('Fetching documents with tag', ..., url=url). Line 129: log.debug('Fetching document', doc_id=doc_id, url=url). Line 196: log.debug('Listing correspondents', url=url). Line 201: log.debug('Listing document types', url=url). Line 206: log.debug('Listing tags', url=url). Every HTTP method logs its URL at debug level, even though the retry decorator already logs on failure and the URL is constructable from doc_id.
findings.suggestion: Remove entry-point debug logs from simple HTTP wrappers. The retry decorator and error paths already provide sufficient observability. Keep info-level logs only for significant state changes (update, create).

findings.summary: Catch clauses for Paperless API calls inconsistently include ValueError — some do, some do not, for equivalent operations
evidence: tags.py get_latest_tags catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError); claims.py catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError) without ValueError; stale_lock.py catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError) without ValueError — all call the same PaperlessClient methods
findings.suggestion: Define a shared PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS tuple and use it consistently across all modules wrapping PaperlessClient calls

findings.summary: daemon_loop.py catches a specific exception list but misses KeyError from malformed API JSON responses
evidence: Line 106: except (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError) — a KeyError from unexpected JSON shape would crash the daemon loop
findings.suggestion: Add KeyError to the exception tuple or use a broader catch with re-raise for SystemExit/KeyboardInterrupt
